macro_architecture.reasoning: 2 active containers (frontend, backend) with 951 total components. Patterns: e2e-xnp: Unknown, jsApi: Unknown, import-schema: Unknown, frontend: Component-Based (Angular), backend: Layered.

layer_structure: Presentation Layer (Components, Directives, Pipes, Guards, Interceptors)
Service Layer (Services, Resolvers, Domain Services, API Services)
Adapter/Data Integration Layer (Adapters, API Modules, Http Clients)

container_analyses.summary: The Frontend container is a component‑based Angular application organized into presentation, service, and adapter layers. It implements a rich set of deed‑management and document‑workflow capabilities through REST integrations, and follows good separation of concerns with high cohesion, though moderate coupling and some architectural debt (excessive services, adapters, and pipes) are evident.

layers: Presentation Layer (Components, Directives, Pipes, Guards, Interceptors)
Service Layer (Services, Resolvers, Domain Services, API Services)
Adapter/Data Integration Layer (Adapters, API Modules, Http Clients)

capabilities: Create, view, edit and delete deeds
Approve and correct deed entries
Manage deed‑related workflows and tasks (archive, reencryption, job execution)
Handle document metadata and PDF generation/display
Import and export data (JSON import, bulk capture)
User authentication, authorization and session management
Configure application settings and key management
Log business events and audit trails
Generate and retrieve reports and analytics
Interact with multiple REST APIs for deed registry, document metadata, tasks, jobs, and reencryption

debt_indicators: Excessive number of services (potential God services)
Large number of pipes (scattered transformation logic)
Potential over‑centralized CoreModule causing hidden coupling
recommendations: Refactor large services into smaller, domain‑focused services or facades
Consolidate similar adapters into generic API client wrappers
Group related pipes into feature modules or replace with reusable utility functions
Review CoreModule imports to ensure only truly shared services reside there
Introduce clear interface contracts between presentation and service layers
Apply feature‑module boundaries more strictly to enforce separation of concerns

container_analyses.overall_assessment: The frontend is well‑structured and implements its domain effectively, but technical debt around service and adapter proliferation and some coupling via the CoreModule should be addressed to sustain scalability and maintainability.
top_recommendations: Refactor large services into smaller, domain‑focused services or facades
Consolidate similar adapters into generic API client wrappers
Apply stricter feature‑module boundaries and review CoreModule imports

layers: Presentation Layer (Components, Directives, Pipes, Guards, Interceptors)
Service Layer (Services, Resolvers, Domain Services, API Services)
Adapter/Data Integration Layer (Adapters, API Modules, Http Clients)

container_analyses.summary: The backend follows a classic layered architecture built on Spring Boot, exposing a rich set of RESTful services for legal document and deed management. While the technical stack is solid and the functional coverage extensive, the current implementation shows moderate coupling and cohesion, with a high number of controllers, services, and entities indicating architectural debt. Quality metrics grade the codebase at a C, highlighting duplication, anemic domain modeling, and insufficient abstractions between layers. Targeted refactoring and better abstractions can raise maintainability, testability, and performance.

capabilities: Create, read, update, delete (CRUD) for deed entries and related entities
Manage actions and action streams
Handle document metadata storage and retrieval
Process handover data sets and history
Apply and manage correction notes
Manage participants and signature information
Perform archiving and signing of submissions
Key management – encryption state queries, re‑encryption, token signing
Expose RESTful APIs for all domain objects
Security and authorization via JSON‑auth integration
Schedule and execute background jobs (e.g., archiving, re‑encryption)
Generate reports and job status information
Health‑check and system monitoring
functional.bounded_context: Legal Document & Deed Management / Archiving System
integrations: Relational Database (via Spring Data JPA / Hibernate)
External Key Management Service (KM) via REST

debt_indicators: Large number of controllers (32) and services (42) indicating possible duplication
Potential mixing of persistence logic in services (tight Service‑Repository coupling)
Very high entity count (360) suggesting anemic domain model
Missing higher‑level abstractions or facades between layers
recommendations: Consolidate related REST controllers into cohesive resource controllers to reduce surface area
Introduce service facades or use case‑oriented application services to limit direct repository usage
Refactor large services into smaller, single‑responsibility components
Review the domain model for aggregate boundaries and consider collapsing or grouping entities where appropriate
Add explicit interfaces for repository and service layers to improve testability and decouple implementations

container_analyses.overall_assessment: The backend is functionally rich and technically sound, but architectural debt and moderate quality metrics hinder maintainability and scalability. Focused refactoring and clearer layering abstractions are required to move the grade upward and reduce duplication.
top_recommendations: Consolidate related REST controllers into cohesive resource controllers.
Introduce service facades / use‑case‑oriented application services to decouple business logic from persistence.
Refactor large services into smaller, single‑responsibility components and add explicit interfaces for better testability.

executive_summary: uvz is a modular monolith system with 951 components across 2 active containers: frontend (404 components, Grade B), backend (494 components, Grade C). Overall architecture grade: C.
